coredata.dc:title: Integrating MXene with carbon dots modified bacteria as hybrid biofilm for photo-assisted microbial fuel cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Shujun
Xu, Ying
Fan, Zeguo
Chen, Suping
Zhang, Shihang
Zhang, Lin
Song, Rong-Bin
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: The development of microbial fuel cells (MFCs) places a high demand on the building of high-performance exoelctrogenic biofilm. In this study, by the electrostatic self-assemble between Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dots-modified exoelctrogenic bacteria (B@CDs), a MXene-based hybrid biofilm has been developed for MFCs. The modification of positively-charged CDs onto bacterial surface not only builds the bridge between negatively-charged MXene and bacterial cells for the construction of hybrid biofilm, but also endows the photoelectric conversion ability for the realization of multiple energy transfer in hybrid biofilm. Meanwhile, the MXene network paves high-speed pathways for electron transfer during microbial catalytic oxidation and photoelectric conversion processes. As a result, the MFC with this MXene-based hybrid biofilm has achieved a maximum power density of 7518.5 mW/m2 in darkness, which increased to 9396.1 mW/m2 under light illumination. This work not only offers a good start for the development of MXene-based hybrid biofilm, but also upgrades the performance of photo-assisted microbial fuel cells to a high level for highlighting their application potential as the green and sustainable energy source.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microbial fuel cells
MXene
CDs
Photoelectric conversion
Hybrid biofilm

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs, B@CDs, MXene/B and MXene/B@CDs
Preparation and characterization of hybrid biofilm

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financial supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22174131, 22274143 and 5200161), Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300421089 and 232300421021), Henan Key Scientific Technological Special Project (221100320200) and Henan Fundamental Research Leading Talent.

This work was financial supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22174131 , 22274143 and 5200161 ), National Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province ( 232300421089 and 232300421021 ), Henan Key Scientific Technological Special Project ( 221100320200 ) and Henan Fundamental Research Leading Talent .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Zeta potential of different samples. Triplicate experiments were performed. (b) Illustration of CDs as a bridge to connect bacteria and MXene. The TEM image of B@CDs (c) and its HR-TEM image (d). The CLSM images of pure bacteria (e) and B@CDs (f) under the superposition of bright field and fluorescence field. SEM images of the pure bacteria (g) and B@CDs (h), the corresponding local enlarged images were shown in the upper right corner and inserted in the lower left corner were corresponding physical images. The SEM image of MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm (i) and its local enlarged image (j).
The FTIR (a) and XRD (b) of different samples. CLSM images of pure bacteria (c), B@CDs (d) and MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm (e). The corresponding enlarged images were shown in the upper right corner.

(a) Photoelectric properties of different anodes/bioanodes. (b) Photocurrent densities of different bioanodes in a three-electrode electrochemical cell. (c) The Nyquist plots with different anodes/bioanodes.

(a) CV curves of different bioanodes under light (solid line) and dark (dashed line) conditions. (b) Discharging curves of two-compartment MFCs with different bioanodes. (c) Polarization curve and power density curve of two-compartment MFCs with different bioanodes under light (solid line) and dark (dashed line) conditions. (d) The power output comparison of MFCs with reported and as-developed hybrid biofilm.
Illustration of the fabrication of MXene/B@CDs and its potential multiple energy conversion mechanism in the fabricated photo-bioanode.

head.ce:title: Integrating MXene with carbon dots modified bacteria as hybrid biofilm for photo-assisted microbial fuel cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Ecology and Environment, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Ecology and Environment, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and their Bioanalytical Applications
Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Ecology and Environment, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A MXene-based hybrid biofilm was developed by electrostatic self-assemble of MXene in the presence of carbon dots-modified exoelctrogenic bacteria, which generated high output in MFC due to the high-efficiency electron transfer pathway and photoelectric conversion ability.

The development of microbial fuel cells (MFCs) places a high demand on the building of high-performance exoelctrogenic biofilm. In this study, by the electrostatic self-assemble between Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dots-modified exoelctrogenic bacteria (B@CDs), a MXene-based hybrid biofilm has been developed for MFCs. The modification of positively-charged CDs onto bacterial surface not only builds the bridge between negatively-charged MXene and bacterial cells for the construction of hybrid biofilm, but also endows the photoelectric conversion ability for the realization of multiple energy transfer in hybrid biofilm. Meanwhile, the MXene network paves high-speed pathways for electron transfer during microbial catalytic oxidation and photoelectric conversion processes. As a result, the MFC with this MXene-based hybrid biofilm has achieved a maximum power density of 7518.5 mW/m2 in darkness, which increased to 9396.1 mW/m2 under light illumination. This work not only offers a good start for the development of MXene-based hybrid biofilm, but also upgrades the performance of photo-assisted microbial fuel cells to a high level for highlighting their application potential as the green and sustainable energy source.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial fuel cells (MFCs) can harness the metabolism of exoelectrogenic bacteria for harvesting electric energy from the chemical energy in organic waste, attracting widespread attentions as a green renewable energy source [1,2]. Nevertheless, the practical process of MFCs is stalled, due to the relative low power density compared to traditional batteries [3,4]. To address this issue, extensive strategies have been explored, in which the modification of anode with nanomaterial is a paramount strategy, as it can enhance bacterial adhesion and extracellular electron transfer (EET) efficiency and give an obvious performance improvement [5,6]. However, when the attached exoelectrogenic bacteria organize a thick biofilm, the redox gradient will be formed, preventing distal bacteria from engaging in EET process [7]. Alternatively, the integration of conductive nanomaterial into biofilm can endow the formed hybrid biofilm with sufficient conductivity for prevent redox gradient [8,9]. Therefore, it is high desirable to develop conductive nanomaterial-based hybrid biofilm for satisfying the need of high-performance MFC [10].

During the building of conductive nanomaterial-based hybrid biofilm, the material selection is a vital step. Compared to other conductive nanomaterials like conducting polymer and metal/metal oxide nanoparticle, the graphene and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are preferred, as the relative large size and the π-π stacking between nanosheets/nanotubes can drive the assemble of crosslinking network throughout the hybrid biofilm for improving the EET and cell density [11,12]. Thus, most hybrid biofilms reported so far have been focused on the use of graphene, CNTs or their composites [13–15]. Recently, MXene, a new type of two-dimensional material, has demonstrated significant potential in energy conversion and storage devices, due to its excellent electrical conductivity, mechanical strength, and chemical stability [16]. With a graphene-like lamellar structure, MXene also possesses the potential as assembly units to build interconnected structure with the assistance of positively-charged linking units [17]. More importantly, MXene displays improved hydrophilicity and biocompatibility compared to graphene and CNTs, which can realize the intimate contact with the redox proteins on the outer membrane of exoelectrogenic bacteria [18]. As a typical example, Liu et al. have found that the modification of MXene improved the biocompatibility between anode and exoelectrogenic bacteria, resulting an improved direct EET and a 82 % increase in the maximum power density [19]. Therefore, the attempt of selecting MXene as the conductive synthetic element for hybrid biofilm is worth to be encouraged, which can promote the diversified development of hybrid biofilm and further improve the performance of MFCs. However, due to the negative charge characteristics of both bacteria and MXene, these two synthetic elements cannot spontaneously organize themselves to form hybrid biofilm [20,21]. To circumvent this limit, a simple but efficient solution is to fully leverage the self-assemble potential of MXene. In this case, it is necessary to turn the electronegativity of individual bacterial cell for severing as linking element during self-assemble process.

Cell-surface modification is a fascinating technology for MFC, in which nanomaterial or proteins has been brought onto the surface of individual exoelectrogenic bacteria [22,23]. Applying appropriate nanomaterial can not only improve the stability of individual bacterial cell, but also alter its surface charge as well as offer additional functionalities, such as conductivity [24,25], photoelectric responsive ability [26,27]. Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoscale carbon-based materials with excellent biocompatibility, conductivity, and stable photoluminescence properties [28]. Through the screening of precursor species, the resulting CDs are easy to display the desired electropositivity [29,30]. The CDs also possess good photo-responsive feature, which has successfully improved the performance of MFCs due to the generation of additional photocurrent under light irradiation [31]. Moreover, even the self-aggregated bacterial cells are formed between MXene nanosheets, the good conductivity of CDs will ensure that the electrons produced by interior bacteria cells can flow out for contributing to the MFC performance [32]. Herein, this nanomaterial was selected as the bacterial surface modifier to prove the possibility of building MXene-based hybrid biofilm for MFCs. As depicted in Scheme 1 , the cetyltrimethylammonium bromide-derived CDs that possessed a positive charge were modified onto the surface of negatively-charged Shewanella Oneidensis MR-1 (S. Oneidensis MR-1). The resulted bacterial cell (B@CDs) induced the self-assemble of Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets through electrostatic interaction, leading to the formation of a highly-confluent hybrid biofilm (MXene/B@CDs). The results showed that the introduced CDs endowed MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm with the ability to produce photocurrent under illumination. Meanwhile, the MXene largely improved the conductivity of MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm, rendering the high-efficiency electron transfer for the improvement of photocurrent and microbial catalytic current. When using MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm in dual-chamber MFC, the maximum power density reached 7518.5 mW/m2 under darkness, which showed an increase of 1877.5 mW/m2 after light illumination. These values reflected a 4.2-fold and 8.7-fold increase when compared to that of MFC with native biofilm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, B@CDs, MXene/B and MXene/B@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized through a meticulous hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.55 g of Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water at 40 °C with sonication, followed by transfer into an oven at 185 °C for 3 h. When the mixture cooled to 4 °C, it was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the residual CTAB. Subsequently, the purified solution underwent freeze-drying to yield a fine white powder as CDs, then kept in dialysis bag in the dark for 2 days for further secondary purification. Finally, the CDs were dried again by freeze-drying technology, and then dispersed in sterile water to obtain a concentration of 1 mg/mL for subsequent use.

Bacterial cells were cultured before the fabrication of MXene/B@CDs. Specifically, 100 μL of S. oneidensis MR-1 was inoculated in Luria-Bertani broth medium (15 mL, 20 g/L), followed by shaking in an anaerobic incubator (150 rpm, 30 °C) for 24 h. After centrifugation (5000 rpm, 5 min), the collected bacterial cells were washed by sterile water for three times, and then dispersed in sterile water to obtain an optical density at 600 nm (OD600) of 1.5. Subsequently, 0.6 mL of as-prepared CDs solution was mixed with 15 mL of as-prepared bacterial dispersion in a disk mixer for 2 h. The resulted mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. After removing the supernatant, the precipitation (B@CDs) was dispersed in sterile water (15 mL) again. Then, Ti3C2Tx MXene obtained by Yury Gogotsi’ methods [33] was dissolved in the water (1 mg/mL), followed by the microwave ultrasound (1 h) to obtain thin-layer MXene. Finally, 0.3 mL of MXene solution was mixed with the as prepared B@CDs solution, followed by the incubation at 30 °C and 150 rpm for 2 h. When the color of the aggregates transformed from light pink to black, the MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm was formed. The mixture obtained by the direct addition of MXene after bacterial culture was MXene/B hybrid biofilm. With the aid of a suction filtration device, these hybrid biofilms and aggregates were subsequently extracted and filtered onto carbon felt (CF, 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm) for fabricating corresponding bioanodes.

The construction of MFCs utilized a standard two-chamber H-type device (28 mL for each chamber) made of transparent acrylic, incorporating a dual-chamber setup with a proton exchange membrane (PEM) (3.5 cm × 3.5 cm, Nafion 211, Dupont Co.). All components, including PEM, electrodes, and devices, underwent sterilization with 75 % alcohol and UV light treatment (1 h) before running. The cathode chamber contained 20 mL of phosphate buffer (PBS, pH 7.4) solution with K3[Fe(CN)6] (50 mM) and CF cathode (2 cm × 3 cm), while the anode chamber was introduced with the mixed solution (20 mL) of M9 buffer solution (3 g/L KH2PO4, 15 g/L Na2HPO4, 5 g/L NaCl and 0.12 g/L MgSO4) and Luria-Bertani (LB) broth (1 % w/w) and the bioanode prepared above. An external 1000 Ω resistor was connected to the anode and cathode, and voltage was recorded by a multimeter. To sustain operation, 25 μL of sodium lactate (0.8 g/mL) was introduced when the voltage dropped below 50 mV, and the process was repeated for three complete cycles. Additionally, N2 gas was injected into the solution before startup to purge dissolved O2.

Electrochemical measurements of the bioelectric energy output from each biofilm anode were conducted in a three-electrode system, ensuring that the setup was shielded from light by wrapping in tin foil and maintained at a constant temperature of 30 °C. For assessment of involving photoenergy-coupled bioelectric energy, all biofilm anodes were firstly tested under dark conditions. Upon achieving stable bioelectricity, the tin foil was removed, and the anodes were exposed to a xenon lamp irradiation of 20 A current for 30 min, preceded by a 10-min initial light avoidance period. After that, the anodes were re-wrapped in tin foil, and 25 μL of 0.8 g/mL sodium lactate solution was added to restore bioelectric energy output. The three-electrode system underwent a total of three operational cycles during testing, employing cooling devices to maintain stable operational temperatures during the light illumination. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) were performed using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument, China). CV tests were performed at a scan speed of 10 mV/s to complement the electrochemical characterization. LSV scans were conducted from the open circuit voltage to 0 V at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. The polarization and power density (P = UI) curves were calculated by the LSV curves, which were normalized by the surface area of both sides of anode (0.5 cm2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hybrid biofilm
ce:section.ce:para: In order to realize the hybrid biofilm concept of bridging structure by electrostatic self-assembly, the CDs with positive charge were first prepared. The precondition for the preparation of CDs was the cationic surfactant CTAB with abundant positively charged groups (CTA+) [34]. As shown in Fig. S1a, the prepared CDs were evenly distributed in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and observed to be circular with a diameter of less than 10 nm. The lattice spacing of the CDs was relatively uniformly dispersed, with a distance of about 0.22 nm. To further verify the establishment of the electrostatic action bridging, the surface charge characteristics of the samples were meticulously examined using a Zeta potential analyser. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the Zeta potentials of bacterial and MXene solutions were measured to be −22.2 ± 1.0 mV and −23.2 ± 0.3 mV, respectively. In other words, there is a strong electrostatic repulsion both in the bacteria themselves and in the combination of bacteria and MXene (Fig. 1b). The Zeta potentials of the as-prepared CDs was measured to be 26.6 ± 0.4 mV, indicating a pronounced positive charge and the potential for electrostatic interaction between CDs and bacteria. This electro-positivity likely stems from the bonding of quaternary ammonium salt groups [R(CH3)3N+Br−] to the carbon core during etherification [35]. Subsequently, the Zeta potentials of the hybrid biofilms were also tested. Upon treatment of CDs, the surface potential bacteria shifted from −22.2 ± 1.0 mV to 15.8 ± 0.2 mV. In the TEM image (Fig. 1c and Fig. 1d), there are obvious punctate particles on the bacterial surface of the B@CDs sample, and the lattice spacing of the punctate particles is 0.2 nm, which is the same as that of CDs. The above characterizations verified that CDs and electrogenic bacteria were combined through electrostatic interaction. After additional treatment with MXene, the surface potential of the MXene/B@CDs solution reduced to 1.3 ± 0.2 mV. This drastically reduction can be attributed to the electrostatic assembly of CDs-modified bacteria with MXene, indicating that the CDs can act as a bridge between bacteria and MXene for solving the as-mentioned electrostatic repulsion (Fig. 1b).

Besides, the CDs have blue-violet fluorescence in the UV darkroom, which provides a simple method for fluorescence detection to verify that CDs are attached to the surface of bacteria. For better optical imaging performance, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was also measured. As shown in Fig. S1b, the emission intensity reached a maximum at 405 nm using the excitation wavelength of 330 nm, and then it gradually decreased as the excitation wavelength increased. Given the capability of CDs to emit fluorescence at specific wavelengths, confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was used to evaluate their fluorescence imaging capability of the samples. In the superimposed field of bright field and fluorescence field with excitation wavelength of 405 nm, the pure bacteria did not have obvious fluorescence (Fig. 1e). However, under the superposition of bright field and fluorescence field, the rod shape B@CDs can be clearly seen, and the fluorescence was obvious (Fig. 1f). These observations provide definitive evidence that the as prepared CDs have been successfully modified onto the surface of electrogenic bacteria for the formation of B@CD.

The morphologies of the prepared samples were also characterized using scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fig. 1g depicts the SEM image of the pure bacterial cells, control group, where the rod-shaped bacteria are dispersed due to the absence of electrostatic attraction. The dispersion state remains unchanged in the B@CDs sample (Fig. 1h), but the color of the bacteria changes from pink to white. Under the effect of electrostatic assembly, the SEM of the MXene/B@CDs reveals a thick hybrid biofilm with cross-stacked 3D network structure (Fig. 1i). As can be seen in the enlarged drawings (Fig. 1j), the bacteria and MXene have formed a tight connection, and the color changed from white to black. The above results further underscored that the controlled assembly was achieved through the electrostatic interactions between B@CD and MXene.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were used to investigate chemical compositions of the as-prepared samples (Fig. 2 a and b). In the FTIR analysis of bacteria, the stretching vibration of 1651 cm −1 is attributed to amide I. Besides, the vibrations at 2922 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1 in the FTIR analysis of CDs are attributed to CH [36]. Therefore, the B@CDs spectrum contains these three stretching vibrations. Particularly, in the spectrum of MXene, the vibration of TiO bond can be account for the peak at 560 cm−1. The result shows that all of these characteristic peaks can be found in the spectra of MXene/B@CDs. The XRD patterns of both bacteria and CDs displayed a broad peak centred at 20°, which was attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms. Due to this overlap, the B@CDs showed a XRD pattern similar to bacteria. The peak at about 6.8° belong to the (002) characteristic diffraction peak of MXene [37]. It is worth noting that the characteristic diffraction peak (002) of MXene/B@CDs is negatively shifted compared to MXene, reflecting the increased layer spacing. This may be attributed to the fact that B@CDs is embedded in the interlayer of MXene, resulting in an increased layer spacing of MXene [38].

Considering that CDs and MXene may possess potential influence on bacteria, the bacterial vitality of each sample was assessed prior to establishing two-compartment MFCs. Specifically, the LIVE/DEAD BacLight Viability Kit consisting of SYTO 9 and propidium iodide (PI) dyes was employed to evaluate the viability of bacteria, B@CDs, and MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm. The dye SYTO 9 stains live cells with intact cell membranes, emitting green fluorescence, whereas the dye PI stains dying cells with damaged cell membranes, resulting in red fluorescence [22]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the CLSM image of the pure bacteria revealed robust green fluorescence with minimal red fluorescence, indicating that the experimental procedures did not compromise the viability of bacteria. After modifying with CDs, the B@CDs sample exhibited a slight increase in yellow fluorescence (superposition of red and green fluorescence) under CLSM in Fig. 2d, however, the majority of viable bacteria still emitted green fluorescence. The slight increase in bacterial mortality could be due to the toxicity of carbon dots towards some weaker cells [39], or it may be caused by the incomplete removal of drug components from the precursors during the purification process of CDs [40]. Compared to B@CDs, the MXene/B@CDs biofilm showed negligible increase in yellow fluorescence, suggesting that MXene exerted minimal impact on the activity of bacteria due to its excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 2e). Fluorescence reduction observed could be attributed to the fact that MXene potentially affected the emission wavelength. The above results suggested that the incorporation involving CDs and MXene had negligible adverse effects on the activity of bacteria. Moreover, the growth curves of native bacteria, B@CDs and MXene/B@CDs were similar (Fig. S2), further confirming the biocompatibility of CDs and MXene for enhancing the functional properties of bacterial cell in MFCs while preserving their cell vitality.

To validate the enhancing effects of MXene and CDs in photo-assisted MFC, we conducted tests on the photoelectric properties of different anodes. Besides, MXene/B mixture was also prepared and tested for contrast, the SEM image of which was shown in Fig. S3. Considering that MXene can increase the conductivity but blocking light absorption of CDs, the optimization experiment of the MXene content in the MXene/B@CDs was firstly carried out, which revealed that the MXene/B@CDs possessed highest photocurrent when the mass ratio of MXene and CDs was 1:2 (Fig. S4a). Therefore, this mass ratio was used for the following MXene/B@CDs. Fig. 3 a shows the current–time curves of different samples in the Na2SO4 solution by switching on/off the light illumination. It is observed that the pure bacteria possess a certain background response, and the current variation was tested to be 0.15 mA/m2 under light and dark conditions [27]. After modifying with photoelectric responsive CDs, the photo-current density of B@CDs bioanode increased by 4.1 times (0.61 mA/m2). However, this value is notably lower than that of pure CDs (Fig. S4b), possibly due to the fact that B@CDs can not assemble to form a well-constructed conductive network for electron transfer. Upon the addition of MXene, the formed MXene/B@CDs further enhances the photoelectric performance into 2.1 mA/m2, which is 14 times of the pure bacteria. Since MXene has almost no photoelectric properties, the reason for its increased current density should be related to its good electrical conductivity and the great cross-linked networks formed in the biofilm. Similarly, the current density variation (0.68 mA/m2) in the MXene/B system was also increased compared to pure bacteria, as the high conductivity enhanced the electron transfer and thus the background response.

Furthermore, we probed the current generation capabilities of these photo-bioanodes in the electrochemical cells (ECs) using three operating cycles (I, II, and III). As shown in Fig. 3b and Table S1, the current–time (I–T) curves of each bioanode exhibited consistent trends in dark. A gradual increase in biological current (biocurrent) from initial levels to a plateau was observed for each bioanode. During substrate depletion, the microbial metabolic rate decreased, consequently lowering the biocurrent. Upon substrate replenishment, metabolic rate was restored, leading to a gradual increase in electrical production [24]. The average biocurrent densities in light (Ilight) of the pure bacteria anode was 2036 ± 283 mA/m2. Compared with the dark condition (1765 ± 491 mA/m2), the current density increase (Inet) was 271 mA/m2, accounting for only 15 % of the biocurrent density in the dark condition. This slight increase in current may be attributed to background signal amplification caused by electron mediators like riboflavin in the electrolyte under light [26]. In contrast, average Idark and Ilight of the B@CDs anode achieved 3188 ± 182 mA/m2 and 4319 ± 673 mA/m2, respectively, with a net increase in current (Inet) of 36 % increment, indicating the good photoelectric conversion ability of CDs. The average Idark and Ilight of MXene/B were 4896 ± 241 mA/m2 and 6021 ± 462 mA/m2, respectively, with an Inet of 1125 mA/m2 and 23 % increment. This may due to the superior electrochemical properties of MXene, which expedited the export of electrons and amplified the background signal. Integrating with CDs and MXene, the plateau current density produced by MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm anode without light illumination is 7244, 6852 and 6147 mA/m2 for each cycle. The average Idark reached 6747 ± 557 mA/m2, significantly surpassing the biocurrent density of the pure bacteria anode. After irradiation, the average Ilight rose to 9643 ± 827 mA/m2, with an Inet reaching 2896 mA/m2, which accounts for 43 % of the maximum current density observed under dark conditions. These results proved that the good conductivity of MXene greatly improved both the electron transport rate during biocurrent and photocurrent generation processes.

The conductivities of these bioanodes were investigated by EIS analysis for getting insight into the high performance generated by MXene/B@CDs. In general, the diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct), and a smaller Rct indicates improved conductivity and interfacial electron transfer efficiency [41,42]. As shown in the Fig. 3c, the Rct of pure bacteria is 926.6 Ω, while the Rct of CDs is 18.6 Ω (Fig. S5). After modifying with CDs, the Rct of the B@CDs bioanode decreases to 257.8 Ω, indicating that the good conductivity of CDs can enhance the electron transfer efficiency of the formed bioanode to some extent. By leveraging the excellent electrical conductivity of MXene (11.3 Ω), the resistance of the MXene/B bioanode experiences a significant drop to 114.1 Ω, even it owns a relative discrete structure. Meanwhile, the Rct of the MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm is merely 31.6 Ω, reflecting an improved electron transfer efficiency. This finding is consistent with the results of the photocurrent experiments. Therefore, the high performance of MXene/B@CDs is resulted by the excellent electron transfer efficiency caused by the introduced MXene, as it can form a three-dimensional conductive network within the biofilm and provide more active sites for timely transmission of bioelectric of the bacteria and photoelectric currents of the CDs.

To further explore the role of MXene and CDs on EET, CV curves of different samples were tested under light and dark conditions (Figs. 4a and S6a). Under dark conditions (dashed line), the CV curve of the pure bacteria bioanode exhibited a pair of redox peaks at −0.448/−0.008 V, consistent with previous reports on the redox characteristic of C-type cytochromes in the outer membrane of S. Oneidensis MR-1 [27]. Similarly, the CV curves of the B@CDs (redox peaks at −0.374/−0.031 V), MXene/B (redox peaks at −0.387/−0.210 V) and MXene/B@CDs (redox peaks at −0.293/−0.192 V) also showed C-type cytochrome electrochemical responses. Notably, the distance between the redox peaks of the MXene/B@CDs (0.101 V) was shorter than that of B@CDs (0.343 V) and MXene/B (0.177 V), indicating accelerated electron transport. This is due to the introduction of highly conductive MXene, which firstly improved the electrical conductivity of the MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm. Moreover, the electrostatic interaction between B@CDs and MXene made the hybrid biofilm compact and built a three-dimensional network structure, further accelerating EET efficiency. Under light conditions (solid line), the reduction peak potential of the MXene/B@CDs shifted positively, while the oxidation peak potential shifted negatively, reducing the peak distance to 0.079 V (Fig. S6b). This shift signified accelerated electron transfer, resulting from the generation of electrons and holes under light condition [27]. Similar reductions in peak sparation were observed in the CV curves of the bioanodes constructed by pure bacteria, B@CDs and MXene/B under light exposure. However, both the net increase values of the oxidation peak current of B@CDs and MXene/B were lower than that of MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm. Thus, the formed MXene network improved electron transfer rate of MXene/B@CDs no matter under darkness or light illumination.

The application potential of MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm was also investigated by assembling it into a photo-assisted two-compartment MFC with carbon felt as cathode. Similarly, the bioanodes fabricated by pure bacteria, B@CDs and MXene/B sample were also used as controls. Fig. 4 b represented the current density-time curves of two-compartment MFCs with these bioanodes. The current density gradually increased until reaching a stable state. However, as the nutrient in the electrolyte decreases, the metabolic rate of the electrogenic bacteria slows down, resulting in a decline in performance. Remarkably, all bioanodes sustained operation for approximately 300 h, indicating the absence of biotoxicity from CDs or MXene. The stable output current density of MFC with MXene/B@CDs bioanode is 5866.7 mA/m2, significantly higher than that of MXene/B (2583.3 mA/m2) and B@CDs (2266.7 mA/m2). This value is 5.2 times greater than that of MFC with pure bacteria bioanode (1133.3 mA/m2). These findings confirm the exceptional electricity generation performance of the designed hybrid biofilm in MFC operation. The polarization curves and the power output curves were utilized to further investigate the capability of bioelectricity generation under dark and light conditions (Fig. 4c). Under dark conditions (dashed line), the voltage output of MFCs with the pure bacteria bioanode exhibited a steep decline as current density increases. In contrast, the other three bioanodes showed a slower decline trend, with MXene/B@CDs demonstrating the least decline. This suggested that the MXene/B@CDs further minimized internal resistance of MFC. After light conditions (solid line), the downward trend of MFC constructed with B@CDs or MXene/B bioanodes was slightly slackened, but the downward trend of MFC constructed with MXene/B@CDs biofilms was significantly weakened. This suggests that the ability of MXene/B@CDs to generate electrons under light conditions and transfer them quickly is the key to improving the performance of photo-MFCs. With regards to power output, the maximum power density in darkness achieved by the MFC with MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm is 7518.5 mW/m2, which is much higher than the pure bacteria (1436.5 mW/m2), B@CDs (4182.6 mW/m2) and MXene/B (5359.2 mW/m2) bioanode. Under light condition, the maximum output power density of MFCs with the MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm reached 9396.1 mW/m2, representing a net increase of 1877.5 mW/m2 compared to the output under darkness. This net-increased value is 8.7, 3.8 and 3.6 times that of MFC with pure bacteria, B@CDs and MXene/B bioanode, showing excellent photoelectrical conversion performance. These results underscored that the network structure constructed by B@CDs-induced self-assemble of MXene can facilitated energy coupling between light and bioelectric energy and accelerated electron transport of bioanode, offering a promising strategy for enhancing MFC performance. Based on these merits, this power density is demonstrating as a superior performance compared to these reported MFCs with the hybrid biofilm fabricated by CNT, graphene or their composites (Fig. 4d) [8,43–48]. Moreover, it was also higher than these of MFCs using CD-modified bacteria or MXene-modified anode, further highlighting the superiority of MXene/B@CDs hybrid biofilm in MFC.

In conclusion, a highly-confluent hybrid biofilm, named MXene/B@CDs, has been successfully prepared for the photo-bioanode of MFC. Surface modification of negatively-charged exoelctrogenic bacteria with positively-charged CDs induced the electrostatic self-assembly of negatively-charged MXene nanosheets for the formation of this MXene-based hybrid biofilm. The result revealed that the CDs endowed this MXene-based hybrid biofilm with good photoelectric property for the conversion of solar energy into electrical energy under illumination. Meanwhile, the MXene was found to reduce the charge transfer resistance of this MXene-based hybrid biofilm, leading to the improvement of biocurrent and photocurrent. As a result, the net power density increase of MFCs with this MXene-based hybrid biofilm after light illumination was 1877.5 mW/m2, representing a 8.7-fold enhancement compared to natural bacteria counterpart. Moreover, even under darkness, the MFC equipped with this hybrid biofilm exhibited a satisfied power output, which exceeded most of MFCs with carbon nanotubes, graphene or their composites-based hybrid biofilm [8,43–48]. This work discloses the potential of MXene in constructing high-performance hybrid biofilms, which will promote the diversified development of hybrid biofilm and push the practical application of various microbial electrochemical systems.

Shujun Qi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ying Xu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zeguo Fan: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Suping Chen: Validation, Formal analysis. Shihang Zhang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Lin Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Rong-Bin Song: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zhaohui Li: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financial supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22174131, 22274143 and 5200161), Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300421089 and 232300421021), Henan Key Scientific Technological Special Project (221100320200) and Henan Fundamental Research Leading Talent.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.02.052.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
